subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Conformity and Normative Social Influence (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study examined whether conformity to peer opinion influences adolescents' aesthetic judgments of visual art.
Participants
One hundred and forty-four high school students in grades 9 through 11 were recruited from two public high schools in a mid-sized city. Parental consent and student assent were obtained. Students who reported a formal background in art (enrollment in advanced art classes or participation in a competitive art program) were excluded to reduce expertise effects. The final sample consisted of 136 students (71 girls, 65 boys), with ages ranging from 14 to 17 years (mean = 15.4, standard deviation = 1.1). Racial/ethnic composition was: 43% White/European American, 24% Black/African American, 20% Hispanic/Latino, 8% Asian/Asian American, and 5% Other.
Method
Participants completed the study in individual cubicles with a laptop computer. During Phase 1 (baseline), each participant rated the aesthetic appeal of 20 abstract digital paintings on a 10-point scale (1 = not at all appealing, 10 = extremely appealing). Participants were told that other students had previously rated the same paintings.
After completing baseline ratings, participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions. In the social influence condition, participants were shown fabricated peer ratings alongside the paintings. For 10 of the 20 paintings, the fabricated peer ratings were substantially higher (by approximately 3 points) than the participant's own baseline rating; for the other 10, fabricated peer ratings were substantially lower. In the control condition, participants saw no peer rating information and were asked to re-rate all 20 paintings after a brief filler task.
In Phase 2, all participants re-rated all 20 paintings. Conformity was calculated as the degree to which participants' Phase 2 ratings shifted toward the provided peer ratings relative to their Phase 1 baseline.
Results and Discussion
The researchers predicted that adolescents in the social influence condition would shift their ratings significantly more than those in the control condition. The results are summarized in the table.

Mean Conformity Shift (in points) by Condition and Direction

| Condition | Shift Toward Higher Peer Ratings | Shift Toward Lower Peer Ratings |
|---|---|---|
| Social influence condition | +1.8 | -1.6 |
| Control condition | +0.2 | -0.1 |

Participants in the social influence condition shifted their ratings significantly toward the fabricated peer ratings in both directions, while control participants showed minimal change. The researchers concluded that adolescents show substantial conformity to peer aesthetic judgments and that social influence effects are roughly symmetric — adolescents adjust their ratings upward or downward in response to peer opinion.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies the research method as an experiment, referencing random assignment or manipulation of an independent variable.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for accurately identifying the research method as an experiment. Acceptable response: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to the social influence or control condition, or because the researchers manipulated the independent variable (exposure to fabricated peer ratings).

sample_responses.response: The researchers used an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to either the social influence condition or the control condition.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the method and uses random assignment as the defining criterion. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: This is an experiment because the researchers manipulated the independent variable — whether participants saw fabricated peer ratings — and observed the effect on their Phase 2 aesthetic judgments.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path focusing on IV manipulation rather than random assignment. Both criteria are acceptable. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers used a survey study because they used rating scales to measure participants' opinions about the paintings.
sample_responses.commentary: The rating scale was a measurement tool, not the research method. The study involved random assignment and IV manipulation — the defining features of an experiment, not a survey. 0 pts.

required_elements: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to the social influence condition or the control condition.
alternatives.correct_example: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to the social influence condition or the control condition.

required_elements: This is an experiment because an independent variable (exposure to fabricated peer ratings) was manipulated and participants were randomly assigned to conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: This is an experiment because an independent variable (exposure to fabricated peer ratings) was manipulated and participants were randomly assigned to conditions.

wrong_examples: The researchers used a naturalistic observation. [Incorrect — it was a controlled experiment.]
This was a survey study. [Incorrect — the rating scale was a measurement tool within an experiment, not the method itself.]
common_traps: Identifying the rating scale as the research method
Confusing a field study with this laboratory experiment

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of conformity as used in the study.

scoring_points.description: States a measurable or quantifiable definition of conformity as operationalized in the study — the degree (in points) to which participants' Phase 2 ratings shifted toward the provided peer ratings relative to their Phase 1 baseline.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for stating the operational definition of conformity as used in the study. Acceptable response: Conformity was operationally defined as the degree (in rating points) to which participants' Phase 2 aesthetic ratings shifted toward the fabricated peer ratings relative to their Phase 1 baseline ratings.

sample_responses.response: In this study, conformity was operationally defined as the change in a participant's aesthetic rating from Phase 1 to Phase 2, measured in the direction of the peer ratings they were shown — that is, how many points their rating moved toward the fabricated peer opinion.
sample_responses.commentary: Precisely captures the directional and quantitative nature of the operationalization. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Conformity was measured as the numeric shift in rating points between the participant's Phase 1 baseline and Phase 2 re-rating, in the direction of the provided peer ratings.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid operationalization that correctly references Phase 1 baseline, Phase 2 re-rating, and the directional requirement. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Conformity means changing your opinion to match others. It was measured by whether participants rated the paintings differently the second time.
sample_responses.commentary: The first sentence is a conceptual definition. The second is too vague — it does not specify direction (toward peer ratings) or the quantification method (change in rating points). 0 pts.

required_elements: Conformity was measured as the change in aesthetic rating points between Phase 1 and Phase 2 in the direction of the fabricated peer ratings.
alternatives.correct_example: Conformity was measured as the change in aesthetic rating points between Phase 1 and Phase 2 in the direction of the fabricated peer ratings.

required_elements: The operational definition of conformity is the numeric shift (in points) from baseline ratings toward the peer ratings presented during Phase 2.
alternatives.correct_example: The operational definition of conformity is the numeric shift (in points) from baseline ratings toward the peer ratings presented during Phase 2.

wrong_examples: Conformity means going along with others. [Conceptual definition, not the study's operationalization.]
Conformity was measured by whether participants changed their rating at all. [Too vague — does not specify the direction or the quantification method.]
common_traps: Providing a conceptual rather than operational definition
Describing conformity without referencing the direction (toward the peer ratings) or the quantification method

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the effect of peer ratings on adolescents' aesthetic judgments.

scoring_points.description: Accurately describes what the means indicate — that participants in the social influence condition shifted their ratings substantially toward fabricated peer ratings (±1.6–1.8 points) while controls showed minimal change (±0.1–0.2 points) — AND explains what this pattern indicates in the context of the study.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for accurately describing what the data indicate about the effect of peer ratings on aesthetic judgments. Acceptable response: The social influence condition produced large rating shifts toward peer opinion (+1.8 toward higher, -1.6 toward lower), while the control showed minimal shifts (+0.2 and -0.1), indicating that peer information substantially altered adolescents' aesthetic judgments.

sample_responses.response: The data show that exposure to peer ratings caused large shifts: participants in the social influence condition moved their ratings about 1.7–1.8 points toward the fabricated peer opinions in both directions. In contrast, control participants barely changed at all (+0.2, -0.1). This pattern indicates that peer information significantly influenced adolescents' aesthetic judgments.
sample_responses.commentary: Cites the specific values for both conditions and both directions, and explains what the pattern indicates about peer influence. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Compared to control participants who shifted by only 0.2 and 0.1 points, the social influence group shifted by 1.8 and 1.6 points toward the peer-provided ratings. This nearly 10-fold larger shift indicates that peer opinion substantially changed how adolescents rated the art.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation comparing the magnitudes and explaining what the difference indicates. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The social influence group showed larger shifts than the control group.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct direction but no data cited and no explanation of what the difference indicates about peer influence. 0 pts.

required_elements: The data show that social influence caused much larger rating shifts than the control condition — adolescents in the influence condition changed their ratings by roughly 1.6–1.8 points on average toward peer opinions, indicating that peer information significantly changed their aesthetic judgments.
alternatives.correct_example: The data show that social influence caused much larger rating shifts than the control condition — adolescents in the influence condition changed their ratings by roughly 1.6–1.8 points on average toward peer opinions, indicating that peer information significantly changed their aesthetic judgments.

required_elements: Compared to the minimal shifts in the control condition (+0.2 and -0.1), adolescents exposed to peer ratings changed their aesthetic judgments by approximately 1.7 points on average, indicating substantial conformity to peer opinion.
alternatives.correct_example: Compared to the minimal shifts in the control condition (+0.2 and -0.1), adolescents exposed to peer ratings changed their aesthetic judgments by approximately 1.7 points on average, indicating substantial conformity to peer opinion.

wrong_examples: The social influence group shifted more than the control group. [Correct direction but no data cited and no explanation of what this indicates.]
common_traps: Noting which group shifted more without citing specific values
Reporting the shifts without explaining what they indicate about peer influence on aesthetic judgment

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline the researchers followed in conducting this study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies at least one ethical guideline actually applied in the study, grounded in evidence from the study description.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying at least one ethical guideline the researchers actually followed, supported by evidence from the study. Acceptable responses include: parental consent and student assent were obtained (informed consent for minors), deception via fabricated peer ratings requires debriefing at study's end, or participation was voluntary (student assent obtained individually).

sample_responses.response: The researchers obtained both parental consent and individual student assent before the study began, following the ethical guideline of informed consent — which is especially important for minor participants.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies informed consent, notes both parental and student forms, and explains the relevance for minor participants. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Because the study involved deception — showing participants fabricated peer ratings — the researchers would have been ethically obligated to debrief participants at the end, explaining the true nature of the peer rating information.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path identifying debriefing as the ethical safeguard required by the use of deception. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers should not have used deception because adolescents are a vulnerable population and may not fully understand what they were participating in.
sample_responses.commentary: Raises an ethical concern rather than identifying a guideline that was followed. The question asks what the researchers did do ethically, not whether their methods were problematic. 0 pts.

required_elements: Both parental consent and student assent were obtained, following the ethical guideline of informed consent for minors.
alternatives.correct_example: Both parental consent and student assent were obtained, following the ethical guideline of informed consent for minors.

required_elements: The use of fabricated peer ratings constituted deception, which would require a debriefing of participants at the conclusion of the study.
alternatives.correct_example: The use of fabricated peer ratings constituted deception, which would require a debriefing of participants at the conclusion of the study.

required_elements: Participation appeared to be voluntary — students assented individually, following the ethical principle of voluntary participation.
alternatives.correct_example: Participation appeared to be voluntary — students assented individually, following the ethical principle of voluntary participation.

wrong_examples: The researchers should not have used deception. [Identifies a concern rather than a guideline that was followed.]
common_traps: Criticizing the use of deception rather than identifying the corresponding ethical safeguard (debriefing)
Identifying a guideline not supported by the study description

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: If students claim the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must address both a way it is and a way it is not generalizable.

scoring_points.description: Explains the extent to which the study is generalizable with a specific population claim AND specific supporting evidence referencing participant variables from the study.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining the extent to which findings may or may not be generalizable with a specific population claim AND supporting evidence from participant characteristics. Acceptable response: The findings may not apply to adults or younger children because all participants were adolescents aged 14–17, or may not generalize to students with art training because those students were excluded.

sample_responses.response: The findings may not generalize to adults or younger children because all participants were high school students aged 14–17. Conformity to peer opinion may operate differently in adults, who tend to have stronger established aesthetic preferences.
sample_responses.commentary: Makes a clear population limitation claim and supports it with the age range from the study. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Generalizability may be limited because students with formal art backgrounds were excluded. The findings may not reflect how adolescents with art training respond to peer influence — trained students may rely more on their own expertise than on peer opinion.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path using the exclusion criterion as the population limitation. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The study may not be generalizable because only 136 students participated.
sample_responses.commentary: Sample size alone is not a generalizability argument. The response makes no claim about which population the findings may or may not apply to, and does not reference any specific participant characteristics as evidence. 0 pts.

required_elements: The findings may generalize to high school students in grades 9–11 generally, but may not apply to adults or younger children because all participants were adolescents aged 14–17.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may generalize to high school students in grades 9–11 generally, but may not apply to adults or younger children because all participants were adolescents aged 14–17.

required_elements: Generalizability may be limited because students with formal art backgrounds were excluded, so findings may not reflect how art-trained adolescents respond to peer influence.
alternatives.correct_example: Generalizability may be limited because students with formal art backgrounds were excluded, so findings may not reflect how art-trained adolescents respond to peer influence.

required_elements: The study may generalize to high schoolers in mid-sized cities but may not apply to private school students or students from rural areas, since participants came from only two public schools in a mid-sized city.
alternatives.correct_example: The study may generalize to high schoolers in mid-sized cities but may not apply to private school students or students from rural areas, since participants came from only two public schools in a mid-sized city.

wrong_examples: The study used 136 students, which may not generalize. [Sample size claim without referencing which population.]
The study is generalizable. [No evidence provided.]
common_traps: Asserting generalizability without citing specific participant variables
Claiming partial generalizability without addressing both directions

parts.prompt: Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the prediction that adolescents in the social influence condition would shift their ratings significantly more than those in the control condition.
parts.scoring_notes: Partial credit (1 pt) for using evidence without full explanation, or explaining without a specific finding. Full credit (2 pts) requires a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND an explanation of how it supports or refutes the prediction.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (partial): Uses specific evidence from the study OR accurately explains the connection between a finding and the prediction, but not both in a fully developed argument.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for using specific evidence from the study OR explaining the relationship between a finding and the prediction — not requiring both. Acceptable partial response: The social influence condition shifted ratings by +1.8 toward higher peer ratings, while controls only shifted +0.2 — supporting the prediction.

sample_responses.response: The social influence condition shifted ratings by +1.8 toward higher peer ratings, while the control only shifted +0.2 — this supports the prediction.
sample_responses.commentary: Cites specific data and notes it supports the prediction but stops short of fully explaining the mechanism or comparing both directions. Earns the partial credit point.

sample_responses.response: The hypothesis was supported.
sample_responses.commentary: No data cited, no explanation. A bare assertion earns no credit. 0 pts.

required_elements: Uses a result from the table in relation to the prediction without a full explanation.
alternatives.correct_example: The social influence condition shifted ratings by +1.8 toward higher peer ratings, while controls only shifted +0.2 — supporting the prediction.

wrong_examples: The hypothesis was supported. [No evidence cited and no explanation.]
common_traps: Citing data without connecting it to the prediction
Explaining the connection without referencing specific values

scoring_points.description: 1 point (full): Uses a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND explains how it supports or refutes the prediction that social influence condition participants would show significantly greater rating shifts than controls.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for using a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND explaining how it supports or refutes the prediction. Acceptable response: The social influence condition produced shifts of +1.8 and -1.6 points versus only +0.2 and -0.1 in the control — supporting the prediction that adolescents in the social influence condition would shift ratings significantly more toward peer opinions.

sample_responses.response: The data fully support the prediction. The social influence condition produced shifts of +1.8 points toward higher peer ratings and -1.6 toward lower peer ratings, compared to essentially no change in the control (+0.2, -0.1). These substantially larger shifts in both directions indicate that seeing fabricated peer ratings caused adolescents to significantly adjust their aesthetic judgments — exactly as the prediction stated.
sample_responses.commentary: Cites all four data values accurately, compares both directions between conditions, and explicitly explains how the pattern confirms the prediction. Earns the full point.

sample_responses.response: The data support the prediction. Adolescents in the social influence condition shifted their ratings roughly 1.7 points on average toward peer opinion, while controls shifted barely at all (about 0.15 points on average). This nearly tenfold difference in shift magnitude confirms the prediction that peer ratings would produce significantly greater conformity shifts.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation using averaged values and a magnitude comparison. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The social influence group showed more conformity than the control group, which supports the hypothesis.
sample_responses.commentary: No specific data cited, no comparison of specific values, and no explanation of how the finding connects to the prediction beyond a generic directional statement. 0 pts for this point.

required_elements: Participants in the social influence condition shifted their ratings toward higher peer ratings by +1.8 points on average and toward lower peer ratings by -1.6 points, while control participants shifted by only +0.2 and -0.1 respectively — supporting the prediction that adolescents in the social influence condition would show significantly greater conformity shifts.
alternatives.correct_example: Participants in the social influence condition shifted their ratings toward higher peer ratings by +1.8 points on average and toward lower peer ratings by -1.6 points, while control participants shifted by only +0.2 and -0.1 respectively — supporting the prediction that adolescents in the social influence condition would show significantly greater conformity shifts.

required_elements: The social influence condition produced rating shifts of approximately 1.7 points on average toward peer opinion, compared to virtually no change (approximately 0.15 points) in the control condition — confirming that exposure to fabricated peer ratings meaningfully changed adolescents' aesthetic judgments, as predicted.
alternatives.correct_example: The social influence condition produced rating shifts of approximately 1.7 points on average toward peer opinion, compared to virtually no change (approximately 0.15 points) in the control condition — confirming that exposure to fabricated peer ratings meaningfully changed adolescents' aesthetic judgments, as predicted.

wrong_examples: The social influence group shifted more. [Too vague — no specific data and no explanation connecting the finding to the prediction.]
common_traps: Citing only the social influence condition data without comparing to the control condition
Noting the correct direction of findings without citing specific values or explaining how the finding confirms or disconfirms the prediction
